sentences.base_language: I have a job interview at a Korean company today.
sentences.grammarNote: This story uses 합쇼체 (hapshyoche) — the formal speech level ending in -습니다/-ㅂ니다. It is used in formal situations like job interviews, presentations, news broadcasts, and speeches. Polite speech (해요체, ending in -아요/-어요) is used with strangers in everyday life.

sentences.base_language: I am Jiyeon Park. Nice to meet you.
sentences.grammarNote: 처음 뵙겠습니다 (cheoeum boepgesseumnida) is the formal way to say 'nice to meet you'. 뵙다 is the humble/deferential form of 보다 (to see/meet), used to show respect to a superior. The -겠- expresses polite willingness or intention.

**자기소개** (jagisogae) = 'self-introduction' — the standard opening request in Korean interviews and formal meetings.
**해 주시겠습니까?** = 'Would you please do...?' — the polite form 주시다 + -겠- (polite future/conjecture) + -습니까 (formal question ending).
This combination is extremely polite and formal, suited to a job interview context.
Note: 주시겠어요? is the equivalent in polite (not formal) speech.

sentences.base_language: I graduated from Seoul University and majored in business.
sentences.grammarNote:
**졸업하다** (joreophada) = 'to graduate from' — takes the object marker 를/을: 대학교를 졸업하다.
**전공하다** (jeongonghada) = 'to major in' — 전공 (major/specialisation) + 하다.
**-고** connects two verb clauses in sequence: 졸업했고 전공했습니다 = 'graduated and majored.'
Speaking in formal -습니다 level throughout an interview reflects professionalism and respect for the interviewer.

**가장** (gajang) = 'most / the greatest' — the standard superlative adverb. Synonym: **제일** (jeil). 가장 큰 = 'the greatest/biggest.'
**장점** (jangjjeom) = strength/advantage — 장 (長, long/superior) + 점 (點, point). Its opposite is **단점** (danjeom, weakness/flaw).
**무엇입니까?** (mueosipmikka?) = 'what is it?' in formal speech. 무엇 (what) + 입니까 (formal copula question). In everyday speech: 뭐예요?

sentences.base_language: My greatest strength is my communication ability.
sentences.target_language: 저의 가장 큰 장점은 소통 능력입니다.
sentences.romanized: Jeoui gajang keun jangjeom-eun sotong neungnyeogipnida.
sentences.literal: My most big strength[topic] communication ability-is.
sentences.grammarNote: In formal speech, -입니다 replaces the polite -이에요/예요: 학생이에요 → 학생입니다. Questions use -입니까?: 학생입니까? The formal level is not used in ordinary conversation but is essential for professional contexts.

sentences.base_language: I always try to listen carefully to colleagues.
sentences.target_language: 항상 동료들의 말을 잘 들으려고 노력합니다.
sentences.romanized: Hangsang dongnyodeului maleul jal deureuryeogo noryeokamnida.
sentences.literal: Always colleagues' words[obj] well listen-trying-to make-effort.
sentences.grammarNote: -(으)려고 노력하다 means 'to make an effort to do': 들으려고 노력해요 = 'I try hard to listen'. The -(으)려고 ending expresses intention or effort toward an action, similar to 'in order to' or 'trying to'.

sentences.base_language: Do you have experience with international projects?
sentences.grammarNote:
**있으십니까?** = 'do you have?' in the formal honorific — 있다 + -(으)시 (honorific infix) + -ㅂ니까 (formal question).
The honorific infix **-(으)시-** is inserted before the ending to show respect for the subject of the verb (here, the interviewee).
**해외** (haoe) = overseas/abroad — 해 (海, sea/overseas) + 외 (外, outside). Used in: 해외여행 (overseas travel), 해외 근무 (working abroad).
**경험** (gyeonghyeom) = experience — a key vocabulary word for any job interview context.

sentences.base_language: Yes, I have worked on two international projects.
sentences.target_language: 네, 두 개의 해외 프로젝트에 참여했습니다.
sentences.romanized: Ne, du gaeeui haoe peurojekteue chamyeohaetseumnida.
sentences.grammarNote:
**참여하다** (chamyeohada) = 'to participate / take part in' — more formal than 참가하다. Used for projects, events, and activities.
**두 개의** (du gaeeui) = 'two (units) of' — 두 (two, native Korean) + 개 (counter for objects) + 의 (possessive/modifier particle 'of').
The particle **에** after 프로젝트 marks the project as the domain of participation: 프로젝트에 참여하다 = 'participate in a project.'
Using **두 개의** sounds slightly more deliberate and precise than simply 두 — appropriate in a formal interview answer.

**언제부터** (eonjebuteo) = 'from when / starting when' — 언제 (when) + 부터 (from).
**근무** (geunmu) = 'work duty / service' — the formal word for being on duty at a job, more specific than 일 (work in general).
**가능하십니까?** = 'is it possible [for you]?' — 가능하다 (to be possible) + -(으)시 (honorific) + -ㅂ니까 (formal question).
This construction asks about feasibility very respectfully — a hallmark of formal business Korean.

**-(으)ㄹ 수 있다** (-(eu)l su itda) = 'can / be able to' — one of Korean's key ability expressions: 시작할 수 있습니다 = 'I am able to start.'
The opposite is **-(으)ㄹ 수 없다**: 시작할 수 없습니다 = 'I cannot start.'
**즉시** (jeuksi) = 'immediately / at once' — a formal adverb. The casual equivalent is **바로** (baro) or **당장** (dangjang).
있습니다 is the formal positive form of 있다 — completing the formal register maintained throughout the interview.
